turns.content: I've been using cargo-fuzz for a while and it just runs the fuzz function, so I thought my Rust port would work exactly like that. But I'm running into issues where the program crashes before any input gets processed. Is there something different about how I need to structure my test harness here?

The difference is that cargo-fuzz wraps everything in a simple entry point, but with afl.rs you have to manually implement an instrumentation-based fuzzing loop using the afl::fuzz! macro or function. You're likely missing the persistent mode setup which keeps track of seeds across iterations. Without that, every run starts fresh and AFL can't learn from previous executions.
Wait, so I don't just write a single function like cargo-fuzz does? It sounds like my existing code structure won't work at all without rewriting everything?

Not necessarily everything, but you do need to integrate the toolchain properly. The key is setting up persistent mode where the fuzzer tracks seeds and mutations between runs. If your current design relies on state that gets reset every time, it will break AFL's learning mechanism because it can't correlate input sequences with crashes.
That makes sense about the seed tracking, but I'm still confused why my simple function isn't triggering any useful fuzzing results. Is there a specific entry point requirement I'm missing?

Yes, you need to ensure your code is compiled in persistent mode and that the instrumentation hook actually captures the inputs being fed to it. If your Rust crate doesn't expose the right traits or isn't linked with the necessary flags for AFL++ instrumentation, the fuzzer won't be able to record mutations effectively.
So if I just add `#[afl::fuzz]` and call my function from there, should it work automatically without any other changes?

Not quite. You need to make sure your Rust types are marked with the Serialize trait so AFL can serialize inputs across processes, and you must handle the persistent mode lifecycle correctly. If you're treating this like cargo-fuzz's simplicity, you'll miss the setup required for mutation tracking.
I see, but what if my existing tests already use ASan triage? Can I run both AFL and ASan on the same codebase without major refactoring?

You can absolutely integrate them. Use ASan to catch memory safety issues first with your Rust compiler's built-in sanitizer, then switch to afl.rs for broader coverage once those are resolved. The toolchains complement each other rather than competing.

Okay, so I should probably start by fixing my serialization setup before worrying about the fuzzing loop itself. Can you give me an example of how to properly initialize the persistent mode in a real Rust crate?

You'll need to set up the fuzzer with flags that enable persistence and ensure your main function is wrapped correctly so it can receive mutated inputs without crashing immediately. The goal is to let AFL track which inputs lead to crashes while you debug using ASan results.
Thanks for clarifying the setup differences, but I'm still worried this will take too much time compared to just running cargo-fuzz tests every day like before.

It does require more upfront configuration than cargo-fuzz's hand-holding, but once you have the persistent mode working correctly, AFL provides far better coverage and crash correlation. The initial investment pays off when you need deep insight into why specific inputs trigger vulnerabilities.
I think I'll start by refactoring my test harness to support persistence and serialization properly before pushing it to CI. Should I keep cargo-fuzz as a secondary check while I set this up?

Yes, keeping both is smart. Use ASan for immediate safety checks with Rust's compiler, then layer in afl.rs once your serialization is solid. This approach gives you the best of all worlds: fast local testing with built-in sanitizers and deep fuzzing coverage from AFL++ when ready.
I'm trying to understand the difference between using afl::fuzz! as an entry point versus just running cargo-fuzz, and I feel like they do something fundamentally different even though both use AFL.

That's a great question. The core distinction is that afl::fuzz! requires your code to be instrumented with the `#[instrument]` attribute from the afl crate, which adds overhead to every function call, whereas cargo-fuzz runs uninstrumented binaries and uses a different approach to track state.
Wait, so if I don't instrument my code with that specific macro, the fuzzer won even start? That sounds like it would break all my existing tests.

Exactly. If you skip the instrumentation step on functions that need to be tracked by the fuzzer, afl::fuzz! will panic immediately because it can't gather the execution traces needed for learning which paths are interesting.
Okay, but what about persistent mode? Is that just a flag you pass in to save state between runs?

Persistent mode is the default behavior when using afl::fuzz! with a binary target. It automatically saves and loads execution history files so the fuzzer remembers which paths it has already explored, but you can disable this if you want to run in fresh mode every time.
I see, but I'm still confused about how this compares to using ASan for triage. Are they completely separate workflows?

They serve different purposes. ASan is great for catching memory safety issues like use-after-free or buffer overflows early, while afl::fuzz! focuses on finding logic errors and resource exhaustion by exploring the execution space.
So I should probably run both? Like ASan first to clear obvious bugs, then move to AFL for deeper exploration?

That's a solid strategy. Many teams use this two-step approach: ASan to filter out trivial memory errors so the fuzzer can focus on finding actual logic vulnerabilities without getting drowned by false positives.

Hey, I'm stuck on my CI pipeline. My tests are failing because the fuzzing suite keeps crashing before it even finishes its run. I thought setting up afl::fuzz! as a simple entry point would just let me drop into persistent mode automatically.

Hey, that's totally understandable given how eager we are to get our tests running. The thing is, afl::fuzz! doesn't magically jump into persistent mode on its own; it just starts the fuzzer process. You actually need to explicitly configure the instrumentation and set up the persistence logic yourself within your custom code before you call that entry point.
Oh, I see. So if I don't handle the persistence manually, afl.rs will just ignore it? Is there a way to bypass my custom setup and let cargo-fuzz handle everything?

Not quite; cargo-fuzz is more about triage and using ASan for quick failure detection rather than full-blown persistent instrumentation. If you need that deep persistent mode, it's better to stick with afl.rs directly and manage the lifecycle yourself instead of relying on a simpler wrapper.
Right, so I should probably focus on implementing my own instrumentation hooks for the basic types like Vec or String? And then just call afl::fuzz! to kick off the fuzzing?

Exactly. You'll need to implement those hooks for your specific data structures so that the fuzzer can mutate them in-place during execution, and then you invoke afl::fuzz! to start the actual fuzzing loop.

Why does this even work this way? I'm used to cargo-fuzz where I just drop a binary and it starts fuzzing immediately, but here I have to manually instrument my code with the afl::instrument macro before calling the entry point. It feels like I'm doing extra work when I could just use a wrapper crate.

You're hitting on exactly why manual instrumentation is necessary here. Unlike cargo-fuzz which wraps your binary in a black-box harness, afl.rs requires you to actually modify the source code using the afl::instrument macro so that the runtime can hook into specific functions like those handling file I/O or network sockets. The core mechanism relies on the instrumented code being compiled with special instrumentation flags so that when the fuzzer runs your process, it can trace exactly which functions are called and measure execution time to decide whether a seed is interesting.
Okay, so I instrument my main function, then call afl::fuzz!() inside the test function? And if that crashes, does it automatically restart with a new seed?

Exactly. You place the afl::fuzz!() macro call in your test function, which is where the actual fuzzing loop lives. When an error occurs during execution, the framework detects it and automatically restarts that specific seed with a new random input rather than terminating immediately. However, there's a nuance: if you want to persist state across runs—like keeping a database open or maintaining session data—you need to enable persistent mode by setting the environment variable AFL_PID before starting the process.
Persistent mode sounds useful, but I'm worried about complexity. What happens if my test function doesn't return a proper result? Does afl.rs catch that?

The framework expects your test function to always return a Result<T, E> trait implementation so it can handle errors gracefully. If you omit the return type or panic inside the macro expansion without proper error handling, the instrumentation will fail to compile because the generated code must match the specific trait bounds required by the AFL runtime. You should ensure every branch in your fuzzer returns either a success value or an appropriate error variant.
Got it, but I'm still confused about how this compares to ASan triage. Should I always run afl.rs before ASan?

Not necessarily. While AFL++ excels at collecting diverse inputs and measuring coverage, ASan is better for catching memory safety issues like use-after-free or buffer overflows. A common workflow is to use afl.rs to gather a broad set of seeds first, then run those specific seeds through ASan to filter out only the ones that trigger actual memory violations, rather than running both tools in parallel which would be inefficient.
That makes sense. But I keep thinking about how AFL tracks coverage. Is there a specific data structure it uses internally to store this?

Internally, the framework maintains a persistent mode database that stores seeds and their corresponding coverage metrics in a structured format accessible via the afl::state trait. This allows you to inspect which functions were executed during previous runs without restarting the entire process, enabling more targeted fuzzing strategies based on historical execution patterns.

Ah! So I can check the state of previous seeds before launching a new run? That's basically what an aha-moment feels like when I realize I don't need to re-execute everything from scratch. The instrumentation data is already there in persistent mode.

Precisely. That's the power of persistent mode combined with AFL++'s ability to track function-level coverage. Once you've collected seeds and verified they pass basic checks, you can reuse that dataset for deeper analysis or specific target functions without redundant execution.
